game_description: Capture all opponent pieces or block them from moving, 2 players.
filter_1_label: Equal or ahead on piece count?

strategies.strategy_detailed: Checkers is a solved game — perfect play by both sides always draws. Develop center pieces first: the center four squares control the most diagonals and maximize mobility. Edge pieces become targets. Classic openings like Old Faithful (11-15, 23-19) contest the center immediately.

Keep at least 2 pieces on your king row for the first 4-5 moves. Surrendering the king row lets opponent pieces pass unchallenged into kings. A piece that reaches king row changes game tempo dramatically — delay that at low cost.

strategies.condition: You can set up a forced jump
strategies.strategy_detailed: Offer a piece as bait where taking it exposes the capturing piece to a counter-jump. Double jumps are powerful — sacrifice one piece to capture two. The exchange is favorable if the second capture leaves you with a better position or piece advantage.

strategies.condition: Opponent setting up a forced jump
strategies.strategy_detailed: Jumps are mandatory in American checkers — you cannot refuse one once offered. Defense means preventing the setup: look 2-3 moves ahead and avoid moves that let opponent force a jump. If multiple jump sequences are legal, pick the one leaving the best resulting position.
strategy_stealth: Pick best jump if multiple legal

strategies.strategy_detailed: Blocked pieces are effectively removed from the game. Keep your pieces spread across 2-3 diagonals so they can support each other and retreat if attacked. A cluster of 3 adjacent pieces is easily isolated and captured in sequence.
strategy_stealth: Each piece needs a retreat square

strategies.strategy_detailed: Kings move in both directions and are worth roughly 1.5 regular pieces. Race to king when promotion doesn't cost material. Avoid promoting pieces that immediately get captured — wait for a safe lane. First player to king usually controls tempo.
strategy_stealth: Race to king without losing material

strategies.strategy_detailed: When even on pieces, avoid exchanges unless they improve your position. Trade pieces that are blocked or poorly placed for opponent pieces that are active. Force exchanges only when they result in a passed piece or a king promotion threat.
strategy_stealth: Trade weak pieces for opponent's active
Force trade only for promotion threat

strategies.strategy_detailed: When behind in material, seek positions where your pieces are mutually protected and hard to attack. Aim for the double-corner defense (pieces on squares 1, 2, 3, 4) — it's nearly impenetrable even against two kings. Draw is a good result when down in material.
strategy_stealth: Draw is good when behind material

strategies.strategy_detailed: Per Chinook's database: equal kings always draw. 2 kings vs 1 is a forced win — drive the lone king off the long diagonal toward the single corner using opposition. 3 vs 2 also wins with correct technique. Material advantage without technique can still drift to a draw.
strategy_stealth: Drive lone king off long diagonal

strategies.condition: You are the lone king vs two
strategies.strategy_detailed: Stay on the long diagonal (squares 1-5-9-14-18-23-27-32) and keep moving. Avoid corners. The long diagonal gives maximum mobility and is hardest to corner you on. Force draws by maintaining central squares and refusing to be trapped.
strategy_stealth: Refuse to be pushed to edge squares
